title: Writing the research claim without overclaiming
learningObjectives: Explain writing the research claim without overclaiming in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: Write only what the experiment measures: coordination can improve a chosen metric under stated conditions without proving that the learned features are the true concepts. This lesson gives you a concrete test for that claim before the course moves to the next abstraction.

intuition.analogy: A toy experiment is a wind tunnel: simplified enough to know the ground truth, but rich enough that the method can fail for the same kinds of reasons it might fail in practice. Write only what the experiment measures: coordination can improve a chosen metric under stated conditions without proving that the learned features are the true concepts.
intuition.analogyLimit: The toy model tests a mechanism under controlled assumptions. It does not predict a gain on language-model activations until that experiment is run.
intuition.text: Write only what the experiment measures: coordination can improve a chosen metric under stated conditions without proving that the learned features are the true concepts.

toyExample.description: Sample known latent concepts, render several noisy partial views per concept, and compare a pointwise SAE with a set-coordinated variant under equal capacity and compute.

steps.result: Specify exactly what writing the research claim without overclaiming should recover, preserve, or ignore.
steps.insight: The intended factor must be known before a toy result can be judged.

steps.result: Use the same data, capacity, training budget, and evaluation examples for both methods.
steps.insight: Otherwise a visible gain may be a resource difference in disguise.

steps.result: Vary the source correlation, noise, view quality, or sparsity and record the first failure.
steps.insight: The boundary of success is usually more useful than the headline score.

visual.question: Which assumption behind writing the research claim without overclaiming changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: Write only what the experiment measures: coordination can improve a chosen metric under stated conditions without proving that the learned features are the true concepts. In practice, write down the input unit, the operation applied to it, and the exact statistic used to score the result. Those are different things. A learned representation can be useful even when the interpretation attached to it turns out to be wrong.

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. Write only what the experiment measures: coordination can improve a chosen metric under stated conditions without proving that the learned features are the true concepts.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: The toy model tests a mechanism under controlled assumptions. It does not predict a gain on language-model activations until that experiment is run.

methodComparison.question: What must be held fixed?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: The ablation identifies which added component caused a gain.

evidence.claim: Write only what the experiment measures: coordination can improve a chosen metric under stated conditions without proving that the learned features are the true concepts.
evidence.note: Course explanation. Use the research library's primary sources for paper-specific empirical claims.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Writing the research claim without overclaiming determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that writing the research claim without overclaiming improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain writing the research claim without overclaiming to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: This phase checkpoint now combines the ideas from the phase.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
